## PAGE 00135: Identifying suspicious usages

- page_id: `191925206`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191925206/Identifying+suspicious+usages
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T15:58:22.256+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > User Guide > Resource Usage Map
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

635500715

635500727

635500713

**On this page**

4

635500716

In the Resource Usage Map application, you can detect three types of suspicious usages: inconsistent version usages, cyclic usages, and unused resources. In addition, you can filter and investigate suspicious usages by using the Filters pane. The sections below explain how to do that.

##### Inconsistent version usages

The inconsistent version usages filter allows you to see which resources use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects. You can investigate every inconsistent version usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.

To investigate an inconsistent version usage

1. In the Filters pane of a resource usage map,click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the inconsistent version usages filter to see the list of all the inconsistent version usages in the map.
2. Select the inconsistent version usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.
3. Click the **Details** button on the top right corner of the inconsistent version usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example map below, *Car Structure library* directly uses version 20.0 of *MD_customization_for_SysML* but other resources used by *Car Structure library* use version 19.0 v9 of *MD_customization_for_SysML* (indirect usage).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of an inconsistent version usage map.

##### Cyclic usages

The cyclic usages filter allows you to see which resourcesuse themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop. You can investigate every cyclic usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.

To investigate a cyclic usage

1. In the Filters pane of a resource usage map,click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the cyclic usages filter to see the list of all the cyclic usages in the map.
2. Select the cyclic usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.
3. Click the **Details** button on the top right corner of the cyclic usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example map below, *MD_customization_for_SysML* uses itself by using *ISO-80000* which uses *QUDV_SysML 1.4* and *QUDV_SysML 1.4* uses *MD_customization_for_SysML* thus forming a usage loop.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of a cyclic usage map.

##### Unused resources

The unused resources filter allows you to see which resources are not used by other resources stored in the Magic Collaboration Studio repository. Thisfilter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191924592 space=MCS2024xR2 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00136: Importing users and groups

- page_id: `191924592`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191924592/Importing+users+and+groups
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-06-19T11:16:22.592+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > User Guide > Admin web applications > Users application
- labels: ['unrestored-unknown-attachment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can import external users and user groups from LDAP servers to Magic Collaboration Studio.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- To import users or user groups at least one LDAP directory must be added to Magic Collaboration Studio (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding and configuring LDAP server' space=''] ).
- Because of the LDAP export limit, you can only import 1000 users at once.
- It is not possible to import users or user groups with the names that already exist as internal.

To import LDAP users or user groups

1. In the Users application, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
2. Select the LDAP server from which you want to import users and user groups and click OK .
3. In the search bar of the LDAP server pane, enter the name of the user or user group you want to import. If you want to get the list of all users stored in the selected LDAP server, type the asterisk sign (*) in the search bar.
4. Select the check-boxes next to the users and user groups you want to import.
5. Optionally click the Set roles button and assign specific roles to the users and user groups to be imported. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding roles' space=''] .
6. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='import.jpg'] to start the import.

After completing the above steps, you will get a notification that the import process has started. If you import a large number of users at once, it may take a while before the import is finished.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Importing users and user groups in the Users application.

When imported users are new, external users are created and the selected roles are assigned to them (see step 5). If you import users that have been imported before, the existing user information is synchronized during the import process but the role assignments stay the same. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Synchronizing users and user groups' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can import external users and user groups from LDAP servers to Magic Collaboration Studio.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98ca8cfd-64c6-41a3-ab0f-ef419be28c73"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>To import users or user groups at least one LDAP directory must be added to Magic Collaboration Studio (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding and configuring LDAP server" /></ac:link>).</li><li>Because of the LDAP export limit, you can only import 1000 users at once.</li><li>It is not possible to import users or user groups with the names that already exist as internal.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To import LDAP users or user groups</p><hr style="" /><ol><li><p>In the Users application, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_users.png" /></ac:image> and select <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import_users_and_groups.png" /></ac:image>.</p></li><li>Select the LDAP server from which you want to import users and user groups and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the search bar of the LDAP server pane, enter the name of the user or user group you want to import.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11e0cbf0-1d36-4c78-a0eb-da879dc20c47"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want to get the list of all users stored in the selected LDAP server, type the asterisk sign (*) in the search bar.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Select the check-boxes next to the users and user groups you want to import.</li><li>Optionally click the <strong>Set roles</strong> button and assign specific roles to the users and user groups to be imported. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding roles" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import.jpg" /></ac:image> to start the import.</li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, you will get a notification that the import process has started. If you import a large number of users at once, it may take a while before the import is finished.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="importing_users_and_groups.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="letter-spacing: normal;line-height: 1.66667;color: rgb(23,43,77);text-align: center;">Importing users and user groups in the Users application.</h6><p>When imported users are new, external users are created and the selected roles are assigned to them (see step 5). If you import users that have been imported before, the existing user information is synchronized during the import process but the role assignments stay the same. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Synchronizing users and user groups" /></ac:link>.</p>
````

## PAGE 00137: Importing users and groups from LDAP

- page_id: `191925056`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191925056/Importing+users+and+groups+from+LDAP
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2024-10-10T12:23:22.346+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > User Guide > Admin web applications > Settings application > Managing LDAP servers
- labels: ['importing-ldap-users', 'importing-ldap-group', 'synchronizing-users', 'synchronizing-groups', 'copy-assignment-in-ldap-form', 'copy-assignment-while-importing', 'assign-role-while-importing']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

5

You can import users and groups after all LDAP server configuration is completed. Magic Collaboration Studio importing also works as a user synchronization mechanism. Existing users will only be synchronized and new users in the Teamwork Cloud Admin will be imported. You can see user data in the **Users application**before choosing to import users. This importing and synchronization mechanism applies to groups as well. Data, including full name, phone, email, and department, will be synchronized.

##### Importing and synchronizing users and groups

To import/synchronize users and groups

1. On the selected LDAP server card click the **Import** button. The **Import LDAP user/group** pane opens.
2. Query all users/groups or specific users/groups and select them.
3. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='import_ldap_users.jpg'] button. New users/groups will be imported or existing users/groups will be synchronized. The existing user information will be updated but the role assignment stays the same.

##### Managing roles

You can assign roles in the same pane, while selecting users and groups to be imported.

###### Assigning roles

To assign a role from one user/group to another

1. On the Import LDAP user/group pane, next to Role assignments , click Set Roles .
2. In the Set roles pane, type a role name in the search bar.
3. Select a role. If it is a custom role, specify scope.
4. Click the Save button to save the changes.

###### Copying assignments

To copy assignment(s) from one user/group to another

1. On the Import LDAP user/group pane, next to Role assignments , click Set Roles .
2. In the Set roles pane, type a username whose assignments you want to copy in the search bar.
3. Select a user/group. The assignments associated with the selected user/group will appear.
4. Click the Save button to save the changes. The selected assignments will be assigned to the target user/group.

##### Troubleshooting

If you cannot synchronize users and/or groups, check the **server.log** file for a message warning about illegal characters. For example:

```text

```

If you see a similar message, allow the use of the character in the **application.conf** file:

```text

```

## PAGE 00138: Inserting content from another document

- page_id: `191940727`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191940727/Inserting+content+from+another+document
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-10-09T18:55:31.136+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Editing and contextualizing > Contextualizing Cameo Collaborator documents
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can include content, such as diagrams and tables, from one document into another. Keep the included content up-to-date by selecting to display its latest version or include thecurrentcontent version to make sure it will stay the same.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To add media to a Cameo Collaborator document:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space=''] .
- You need the Read Resources permission or Resource Reviewer role for the document.
- You need the Read Resources and Edit Resources permission or Resource Contributor role for the project with read-write permissions for the project branch.

To insert content from another Cameo Collaborator document

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening a resource' space=''].
2. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the bottom right corner of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Content pane' space=''].
3. Click the **Insert from document** icon in the location of an open document section where you want to include content. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the open wizard, select the document from which you want to insert the content.
5. In the next step, navigate to and select the document section from which you want to insert the content.
6. Select the element of the section you want to insert. You can only insert tables and diagrams.
7. Select one of the following insert settings:
  - Display latest version - automatically updates the inserted content to the latest version when it becomes available.
  - Display current version (at the time when inserting) - always displays the content version that was available at the time of insertion. [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_settings.png']
8. Click the Add button.
9. Repeat steps 3 to 8 to insert more content or click [ATTACHMENT filename='cancel_editing.png'] on the bottom right corner of the content pane to exit the editing mode.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191924179 space=MCS2024xR2 version=7 -->
## PAGE 00139: Installation on Linux

- page_id: `191924179`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191924179/Installation+on+Linux
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2026-03-04T15:28:10.069+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > Deployment Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio Installation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following sections describe the full deployment of Magic Collaboration Studio on a single Linux server. The deployment includes installation of third-party components: Apache Cassandra database and FlexNet license server. A set of installation scripts are provided to streamline the installation process. This type of all-in-one deployment should be used for testing and evaluation. For a performant system, we recommend distributing components and services on multiple servers.

The following Linux packages are required for each service. All these packages are automatically pulled from online repositories when using the included installation scripts. For offline installation, refer to the specific installation sections for more information on accessing these packages. Also refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='System requirements' space=''] section for version information.

| Magic Collaboration Studio |
| --- |
| Open JDK Java (see )Linux font package: dejavu-serif-fonts |
| Apache Cassandra |
| Open JDK Java (see )Cassandra and Cassandra ToolsLinux package: chkconfig |
| FlexNet 11.19 |
| Existing Java installation (see )Linux utility package: unzipFlexNet 64-bit Installer and UtilitiesCameo Daemon for FlexNet |

**Prior to installation:**

- Make sure that your hardware and software meet the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='System requirements' space=''].
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Preparing the operating system' space='']Prepare the Linux system for Cassandra database deployment.
- Download and extract the installation script package: *[ATTACHMENT filename='TWC_MCS_install_scripts_2024xRefresh2.zip']*
  - Extract the package in the same location as the installer .bin file
  - Add execute permission to all script files

The provided installation scripts are designed to streamline the installation process and automate system configuration. These scripts require remote access to official online repositories by default. Offline installation is possible if prerequisites are available locally on the system. For more information, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Magic Collaboration Studio FAQ' space=''].

To install and configure a working instance of Magic Collaboration Studio, follow the guides below:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Apache Cassandra' space=''] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing the FlexNet server' space=''] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Magic Collaboration Studio services' space=''] .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Magic Collaboration Studio installation and configuration on a single Linux server.

## PAGE 00140: Installation on Windows

- page_id: `191924189`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191924189/Installation+on+Windows
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-10-04T15:59:39.103+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > Deployment Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio Installation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Magic Collaboration Studio installation and configuration on a single Windows server.

This section provides instructions on how to install Cassandra and Magic Collaboration Studio on Windows. During the installation, the installer asks you to provide the required configuration values and creates a Windows service for you.

After completing the installation, make sure you grant access through the firewall to the required ports, as shown in the diagram above.

Once the installation is complete, you need to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying Magic Collaboration Studio license' space='MCS2024xR2'] to start working with Magic Collaboration Studio in MagicDraw and using Teamwork Cloud Admin.

To install and set up Magic Collaboration Studio on Windows, complete the following steps:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='FlexNet license server installation and licensing' space='IL2024xR2'] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Cassandra on Windows' space='MCS2024xR2'] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Magic Collaboration Studio and services on Windows' space='MCS2024xR2'] .
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Starting Magic Collaboration Studio on Windows' space='MCS2024xR2'] .

## PAGE 00141: Installation using scripts

- page_id: `218759799`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/218759799/Installation+using+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-03-18T14:15:47.741+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > Deployment Guide > Monitoring stack
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

5

**Downloads**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh2.zip']

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves setting up a monitoring node and at least one telemetry node. The monitoring node consists of a database for storing metrics, JMX interface for communication with Teamwork Cloud services, and web interface for realtime data visualization. The telemetry node installs server agents for streaming metrics to the monitoring node. We recommend deploying the monitoring node on a dedicated server. Single-node deployment of the Monitoring Stack and Teamwork Cloud is possible, provided there is enough system resources for all components.

#### Installing Monitoring Stack

**Single-node deployment**

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves downloading the package, running the installation scripts on all nodes, and configuring visualization.

1. Download and extract *[ATTACHMENT filename='monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh2.zip']*.
2. Add execute permission to all .sh files extracted, if they are not set.
3. Navigate to the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder.
4. Execute *install_monitoring_node.sh* inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).
5. Stop **twcloud**service.
6. Navigate to the *Telemetry-Node-Install* folder.
7. Execute *install_telemetry_node.sh.*
8. Restart **cassandra**service.
9. Start **twcloud**service.

**Multi-node deployment**

Use*install_monitoring_node.sh* to install the monitoring components and configure one telemetry node.

Then, use *add_twc_monitor.sh*to add each additional telemetry node. These telemetry nodes can be a Teamwork Cloud node or dedicated Cassandra node.

Finally, run *install_telemetry.sh* on each telemetry node.

1. Download and extract *[ATTACHMENT filename='monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh2.zip']*.
2. Transfer the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder to the monitoring node.
3. Navigate to the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder.
4. Execute *install_monitoring_node.sh*inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).
  - Provide the address for one of the telemetry nodes during installation.
5. After installation script finishes, execute *add_twc_monitor.sh* to add additional telemetry nodes.
  - One additional telemetry node will be added with each execution of *add_twc_monitor.sh.*
6. Transfer the *Telemetry-Node-Install*folder to each telemetry node.
7. On each telemetry node:
  1. Navigate to the Telemetry-Node-Install folder.
  2. Stop **twcloud**service.
  3. Execute *install_telemetry_node.sh* inside this folder.
  4. Restart **cassandra**service.
  5. Start **twcloud**service.

## PAGE 00142: Installing Apache Cassandra

- page_id: `191924183`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191924183/Installing+Apache+Cassandra
- version_number: 10
- version_date: `2026-04-16T09:26:00.521+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > Deployment Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio Installation > Installation on Linux
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

Magic Collaboration Studio uses Apache Cassandra, an open-source NoSQL distributed database. Before installing Magic Collaboration Studio, please follow the steps below to install Apache Cassandra.

##### Prerequisites

- OpenJDK Java (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] for version information)
- Apache Cassandra RPMs
  - cassandra- <version_number> .noarch.rpm
  - cassandra-tools- <version_number> .noarch.rpm

- chkconfig package (for service startup on boot)

##### Installing with script

The script downloads and installs the necessary packages, Cassandra, and the Cassandra tools from the Apache Software Foundation repository, and creates the necessary firewall rules to allow proper operation both for a single node or a cluster installation. The script will also install Java 11 and set it as the default system Java.

The script can also be used for offline installation, download the prerequisite RPM packages and place them in the same location as the installation script. Manually install Java 11 and set it as the system default Java. Installation with Apache Cassandra RPM still uses the older System V init to automate service startup on boot. In order to enable the Cassandra service with systemctl, installation of the chkconfig package is needed.

To install Apache Cassandra

1. Install Apache Cassandra by executing the *install_cassandra<version_number>_ol_rhel.sh* installation script. Exampletrue_ol_rhel.sh]]>
2. Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
3. Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are "UN", indicating that the node status isUp, and its state isNormal. bash Wait for a few minutes until Cassandra starts for the first time before checking if it is running. If Cassandra has not started yet, you will get the error: "No nodes present in the cluster. Has this node finished starting up?" This means that you need to give Cassandra more time to start.
4. If Apache Cassandra is not running or if you used installation options other than the one described in this chapter, optionally .

##### Developing a backup strategy

Before deploying Magic Collaboration Studio and Apache Cassandra in a production environment, it is imperative to have a fully implemented backup strategy. The Cassandra database stores all project and user data associated with Magic Collaboration Studio. Review the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Backup and restore data procedures' space=''] document. Ensure that you test the entire backup and restore process before your deployment goes live to users.

During the backup process, user access to Cassandra should be suspended. Refer to the [Cassandra backup documentation](https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/stable/cassandra/operating/backups.html) for more information.

Improper backup procedure can lead to total data loss! For example, taking an image snapshot of the storage system while Cassandra is actively accepting read and write requests will result in unrecoverable data.

##### Configuring Apache Cassandra for Magic Collaboration Studio

If you used other installation options and not the provided script or if Apache Cassandra does not start, configure it as described below.

install_cassandra<version_number>_<os_version>.sh

To configure Apache Cassandra

1. Edit the *cassandra.yaml* file by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
2. Find the following parameters related to the Cassandra node IP address and communication settings, and change them as shown below: bashExample
  - seeds - a comma-delimited list containing all of the seeds in the Cassandra cluster. Since our cluster consists of a single node, it contains only one entry - our IP address.
  - listen address - the IP address that Cassandra uses to listen for connections.
  - broadcast_rcp_address - the IP address used to broadcast to other Cassandra nodes in the cluster. This parameter may be commented. In such case, remove "#" and make sure there are no leading spaces.
  - rcp_address - when set to to 0.0.0.0, Cassandra listens to rpc requests on all interfaces.
3. Find the following parameters that control thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly, and change them as shown below: bashExample
4. To ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended), uncomment the**commitlog_total_space_in_mb** parameter as show as below. bashExample Ensure that the partition where the commit log is installed has enough space to accommodate a commit log of 8GB.
5. To point the data to the appropriate locations, find the following parameters and change them as shown below: bashExample
6. Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
7. Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are "UN", indicating that the node status isUp, and its state isNormal. bashExample

##### Configuring Cassandra memory usage

If you did not use the installation script or want to increase the RAM usage by Cassandra, make the following changes. Otherwise, these configuration changes are set automatically by the Cassandra installation script.

Configuration files are located in */etc/cassandra/conf/*.

- By default, the maximum RAM usage for Cassandra is 8GB. To change the amount of RAM used by Cassandra, uncomment **-Xms4G (min)** and **-Xmx4G (max)** in the *jvm-server.options* file and specify their values.
- In the jvm11-server.options and jvm8-server.options files, comment all lines from"### CMS Settings" to "### G1 Settings".
- In the *jvm11-server.options* and *jvm8-server.options* files, uncomment the following lines:
- In the *jvm11-server.options* and *jvm8-server.options* files, uncomment the following lines and set the values to the physical CPU core count (the values of both parameters should be the same):
- In the jvm8-server.options file, comment all lines from "### GC logging options" to the end of the file.
- Synchronize CPU clocks on all Cassandra cluster nodes. Otherwise, you may encounter issues when creating an empty Cassandra cluster.
- When using cqlsh, use Python 3.6.0 or a later version. Python 2.7 series is no longer supported.
- In the logback.xml file, comment the "<appender-ref ref="ASYNCDEBUGLOG" />" line. This will increase Cassandra's performance by disabling the debug log.

##### Configuring Linux environment for Cassandra performance

If you install Magic Collaboration Studio using the *install_twc_mcs_centos_rhel.sh* script, Cassandra performance is tuned automatically. However, if you plan to use other installation options or if you need to set other parameters after running the script, you can do it manually as described in this section.

To improve Apache Cassandra performance

1. Open the *sysctl.conf* file by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
2. To configure the TCP settings, add the following tuning parameters to the file: bashExample
3. To apply the setting without rebooting, execute the following command: bashDJangotrue

For more information about tuning Linux, see [DSE 6.8 Administrator Guide](https://docs.datastax.com/en/dse/6.8/dse-admin/datastax_enterprise/config/configRecommendedSettings.html).

###### Using jemalloc memory allocator

The jemalloc memory allocator package can potentially improve Cassandra performance. Our installation script does not install jemalloc. The easiest way to install this optional package is to first install the epel-release package. You can then pull the latest jemalloc release from the EPEL repository. An older version jemalloc is available for direct download and install.

- EPEL package (optional, for pulling jemalloc package)

- jemalloc package (optional for performance)

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0943308d-2380-4fac-a8e7-3f59b4ff476c"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Magic Collaboration Studio uses Apache Cassandra, an open-source NoSQL distributed database. Before installing Magic Collaboration Studio, please follow the steps below to install Apache Cassandra.</p><h3>Prerequisites</h3><ul><li data-uuid="e83dabee-21ff-4086-a1b0-47d656f93a4a">OpenJDK Java (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Third-Party Component Compatibility" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Third Party Component Compatibility]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span> </span>for version information)</li><li data-uuid="b19cf371-6dcf-491e-b7d9-d385cc6e81a5"><a href="https://apache.jfrog.io/ui/native/cassandra-rpm">Apache Cassandra RPMs</a><ul><li>cassandra-<em>&lt;version_number&gt;</em>.noarch.rpm</li><li>cassandra-tools-<em>&lt;version_number&gt;</em>.noarch.rpm</li></ul></li></ul><ul><li data-uuid="c5890614-1678-4041-bc2b-353e491d8f62"><p>chkconfig package (for service startup on boot)</p></li></ul><h3>Installing with script</h3><p><span>The script downloads and installs the necessary packages, Cassandra, and the Cassandra tools from the Apache Software Foundation repository, and creates the necessary firewall rules to allow proper operation both for a single node or a cluster installation. The script will also install Java 11 and set it as the default system Java. </span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">The script can also be used for offline installation, download the prerequisite RPM packages and place them in the same location as the installation script. Manually install Java 11 and set it as the system default Java. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Installation with Apache Cassandra RPM still uses the older System V init to automate service startup on boot. In order to enable the Cassandra service with systemctl, installation of the chkconfig package is needed.</span></p><p>To install Apache Cassandra</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Install Apache Cassandra by executing the <em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">install_cassandra&lt;version_number&gt;_ol_rhel.sh</span></em> installation script.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f9db5eb9-7d58-4f2d-bff6-208ab1367f1c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo ./install_cassandra<version_number>_ol_rhel.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="01c42391-f7eb-423f-9c6e-451e6ca01c9d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo systemctl start cassandra]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5613508b-05f5-4909-9693-2d30058d0b68"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool status]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are &quot;</span>UN<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">&quot;, indicating that the node status is </span>U<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">p, and its state is </span>N<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">ormal.</span></p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af89f7fc-42c7-4111-99cc-6594794a2117"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Datacenter: datacenter1
=======================
Status=Up/Down
|/ State=Normal/Leaving/Joining/Moving
--  Address    Load       Tokens   Owns (effective)  Host ID                               Rack
UN  127.0.0.1  128.4 KB   256      100.0%            ea3f99eb-c4ad-4d13-95a1-80aec71b750f  rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38453dcd-5cb6-45ac-a7e8-d10f67d7deef"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Wait for a few minutes until Cassandra starts for the first time before checking if it is running. If Cassandra has not started yet, you will get the error: &quot;No nodes present in the cluster. Has this node finished starting up?&quot; This means that you need to give Cassandra more time to start.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>If Apache Cassandra is not running or if you used installation options other than the one described in this chapter, optionally <ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring Apache Cassandra (optional)"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[configure Apache Cassandra]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h3>Developing a backup strategy</h3><p>Before deploying Magic Collaboration Studio and Apache Cassandra in a production environment, it is imperative to have a fully implemented backup strategy. The Cassandra database stores all project and user data associated with Magic Collaboration Studio. Review the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Backup and restore data procedures" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[backup and restore data procedure]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> document. Ensure that you test the entire backup and restore process before your deployment goes live to users.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d332610a-ceff-4e66-85c4-f5b61ab0b956"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>During the backup process, user access to Cassandra should be suspended. Refer to the <a href="https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/stable/cassandra/operating/backups.html">Cassandra backup documentation</a> for more information.</span><span> </span></p><p>Improper backup procedure can lead to total data loss! For example, taking an image snapshot of the storage system while Cassandra is actively accepting read and write requests will result in unrecoverable data.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h3>Configuring Apache Cassandra for Magic Collaboration Studio</h3><p>If you used other installation options and not the provided script or if Apache Cassandra does not start, configure it as described below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54cf37b4-fecc-428a-b542-171d628f71bb"><ac:rich-text-body>Before starting, note that you do not need to configure Apache Cassandra if you installed it using the installation script we provided (<em>install_cassandra&lt;version_number&gt;_&lt;os_version&gt;.sh</em>). It should start without any additional configuration.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To configure Apache Cassandra</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Edit the <em>cassandra.yaml</em> file by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d775661d-8baa-4497-89c2-854a6800b726"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo nano /etc/cassandra/default.conf/cassandra.yaml]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Find the following parameters related to the Cassandra node IP address and communication settings, and change them as shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b6a9752-4694-4d9c-ab86-1b951b51e430"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seeds: "192.168.130.10"
listen_address: 192.168.130.10
broadcast_rpc_address: 192.168.130.10
rpc_address: 0.0.0.0]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><strong>seeds</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">a comma-delimited list containing all of the seeds in the Cassandra cluster. Since our cluster consists of a single node, it contains only one entry - our IP address.</span></li><li><strong>listen address</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the IP address that Cassandra uses to listen for connections.</span></li><li><strong>broadcast_rcp_address</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the IP address used to broadcast to other Cassandra nodes in the cluster. This parameter may be commented. In such case, remove &quot;#&quot; and make sure there are no leading spaces.</span></li><li><strong>rcp_address</strong> - when set to <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to </span>0.0.0.0, Cassandra listens <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to rpc requests on all interfaces.</span></li></ul></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Find the following parameters that <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">control thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly, and change them as shown below:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4cb4630-dcd0-428b-a2e8-b52de1f74a28"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_segment_size: 192MiB
read_request_timeout: 1800000ms
range_request_timeout: 1800000ms
write_request_timeout: 1800000ms
cas_contention_timeout: 1000ms
truncate_request_timeout: 1800000ms
request_timeout: 1800000ms
batch_size_warn_threshold: 3000KiB
batch_size_fail_threshold: 5000KiB]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended), uncomment the </span><strong>commitlog_total_space_in_mb</strong> parameter as <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">show as below.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d71783fa-d7d3-4d31-9f6c-2b987cbe5147"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_total_space: 8192MiB]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cff29604-57d5-48a5-8e32-b1435084a97c"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Ensure that the partition where the commit log is installed has enough space to accommodate a commit log of 8GB.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To point the data to the appropriate locations, find the following parameters and change them as shown below:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5657b9ba-2980-4ecc-a772-a8c5642c99c3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[data_file_directories:
- /data/data
commitlog_directory: /logs/commitlog
hints_directory: /data/hints
saved_caches_directory: /data/saved_caches]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="489be1f4-0a8b-4c6e-b865-bed3f911251a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo systemctl start cassandra]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="592c72ee-1452-4673-b3d6-1db92d1e6336"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool status]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are &quot;</span>UN<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">&quot;, indicating that the node status is </span>U<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">p, and its state is </span>N<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">ormal.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dec272aa-e4fa-47a2-80ca-5fb891e9ae13"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Datacenter: datacenter1
=======================
Status=Up/Down
|/ State=Normal/Leaving/Joining/Moving
--  Address    Load       Tokens   Owns (effective)  Host ID                               Rack
UN  127.0.0.1  128.4 KB   256      100.0%            ea3f99eb-c4ad-4d13-95a1-80aec71b750f  rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><h3>Configuring Cassandra memory usage</h3><p class="auto-cursor-target">If you did not use the installation script or want to increase the RAM usage by Cassandra, make the following changes. Otherwise, these configuration changes are set automatically by the Cassandra installation script.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc5f84aa-7fdb-4c4f-aabf-15667fd60303"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Configuration files are located in <em>/etc/cassandra/conf/</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">By default, the maximum RAM usage for Cassandra is 8GB. To change the amount of RAM used by Cassandra, uncomment <strong>-Xms4G (min)</strong> and <strong>-Xmx4G (max)</strong> in the <em>jvm-server.options</em> file and specify their values.</span></li><li>In the <em>jvm11-server.options</em> and <em>jvm8-server.options</em> files, comment all lines from&quot;### CMS Settings&quot; to &quot;### G1 Settings&quot;.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <em>jvm11-server.options</em> and <em>jvm8-server.options</em> files, uncomment the following lines:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="285ad60f-a05b-4542-8840-20a9bf740553"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#-XX:+UseG1GC
#-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=500]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <em>jvm11-server.options</em> and <em>jvm8-server.options</em> files, uncomment the following lines and set the values to the physical CPU core count (the values of both parameters should be the same):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="655e3936-4277-4fee-aa05-fccf7d45373d"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#-XX:ParallelGCThreads=8
#-XX:ConcGCThreads=8]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>In the <em>jvm8-server.options</em> file, comment all lines from &quot;### GC logging options&quot; to the end of the file.</li><li><p>Synchronize CPU clocks on all Cassandra cluster nodes. Otherwise, you may encounter issues when creating an empty Cassandra cluster.</p></li><li>When using cqlsh, use Python 3.6.0 or a later version. Python 2.7 series is no longer supported.</li><li>In the logback.xml file, comment the &quot;&lt;appender-ref ref=&quot;ASYNCDEBUGLOG&quot; /&gt;&quot; line. This will increase Cassandra's performance by disabling the debug log.</li></ul><h3>Configuring Linux environment for Cassandra performance</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fcce1a17-001e-4c1b-9e30-ef82b8f277d8"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you install Magic Collaboration Studio using the <em>install_twc_mcs_centos_rhel.sh</em> script, Cassandra performance is tuned automatically. However, if you plan to use other installation options or if you need to set other parameters after running the script, you can do it manually as described in this section.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To improve Apache Cassandra performance</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Open the <em>sysctl.conf</em> file by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c9fbe2b-1f6f-4cab-81bc-7a7e723b9be4"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo nano /etc/sysctl.conf]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To configure the TCP settings, add the following tuning parameters to the file:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00737da0-3dba-4b4c-9e5b-dd7b56cba87d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[net.core.rmem_max=16777216
net.core.wmem_max=16777216
net.core.optmem_max=40960
net.core.default_qdisc=fq
net.core.somaxconn=4096
net.ipv4.conf.all.arp_notify = 1
net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time=60
net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_probes=3
net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_intvl=10
net.ipv4.tcp_mtu_probing=1
net.ipv4.tcp_rmem=4096 12582912 16777216 
net.ipv4.tcp_wmem=4096 12582912 16777216 
net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=8096
net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle = 0
net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse = 1 
vm.max_map_count = 1048575
vm.swappiness = 0
vm.dirty_background_ratio=5
vm.dirty_ratio=80
vm.dirty_expire_centisecs = 12000]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To apply the setting without rebooting, execute the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="401860c9-24af-44d0-b43a-0d1df77494d2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo sysctl -p]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p>For more information about tuning Linux, see <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/dse/6.8/dse-admin/datastax_enterprise/config/configRecommendedSettings.html">DSE 6.8 Administrator Guide</a>.</p><h4><span>Using jemalloc memory allocator</span></h4><p><span>The jemalloc memory allocator package can potentially improve Cassandra performance. Our installation script does not install jemalloc. The easiest way to install this optional package is to first install the epel-release package. You can then pull the latest jemalloc release from the EPEL repository. An older version jemalloc is available for direct download and install.</span></p><ul><li>EPEL package (optional, for pulling jemalloc package)</li></ul><ul><ul><li>Repository pull (epel-release)</li><li><a href="https://dl.fedoraproject.org/pub/epel/epel-release-latest-8.noarch.rpm">RHEL 8 direct download</a></li></ul></ul><ul><li>jemalloc package (optional for performance)</li></ul><ul><ul><li>EPEL Repository pull for latest version (jemalloc)</li><li><a href="https://repo.percona.com/yum/release/8/RPMS/x86_64/jemalloc-3.6.0-1.el8.x86_64.rpm">Older version (3.6) for direct download</a></li></ul></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191925238 space=MCS2024xR2 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00143: Installing Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin

- page_id: `191925238`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191925238/Installing+Cameo+Collaborator+Publisher+plugin
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-10-04T16:00:49.282+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Publishing documents
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To publish models to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, install the Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin in your modeling tool.

#### WARNING: Compatibility

Compatibility

- The Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin is only compatible with the same version of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.
- Make sure the Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin version is compatible with the version of your modeling tool. See the compatibility table .

For information regarding installation, licensing, and system requirements, visit the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installation, licensing, and system requirements' space='IL2024xR2']page.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing from graphical user interface' space='MCS2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing from command line' space='MCS2024xR2']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>To publish models to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, install the Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin in your modeling tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e30a40f-77ee-403b-876b-18c68c81d94f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Compatibility</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>The Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin is only compatible with the same version of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.</li><li>Make sure the Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin version is compatible with the version of your modeling tool. See the <a href="https://www.nomagic.com/support/compatibility#Cameo%20Collaborator%20Publisher%20plugin">compatibility table</a>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">For information regarding installation, licensing, and system requirements, visit the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL2024xR2" ri:content-title="Installation, licensing, and system requirements" /></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> page.</span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Publishing from graphical user interface" ri:space-key="MCS2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Publishing from command line" ri:space-key="MCS2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191924197 space=MCS2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00144: Installing Cassandra on Windows

- page_id: `191924197`
- space_key: `MCS2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024xR2/pages/191924197/Installing+Cassandra+on+Windows
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T15:58:11.194+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio > Deployment Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio Installation > Installation on Windows
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

**Downloads**

[ATTACHMENT filename='CustomTWC_Cassandra4_DockerImg.zip']

#### WARNING: Important! Cassandra is not supported on Windows!

Important! Cassandra is not supported on Windows!

We do not provide support for Cassandra installations on Windows as those installations are not natively supported by Apache. **We strongly recommend using Linux deployments.**The information below is provided as a courtesy only.

One option for running Cassandra 4.1 on Windows is Docker. See [Install Docker Desktop on Windows](https://docs.docker.com/desktop/install/windows-install/) for official installation instructions.

Additional resources:

- https://hub.docker.com/_/cassandra/ – official Cassandra Docker image
- https://docs.docker.com/ – Docker documentation
- https://github.com/apache/cassandra/blob/cassandra-4.1/NEWS.txt – Cassandra’s migration notes

##### Docker Cassandra basics

This section provides the basics for starting a Docker Cassandra 4.1 container, for testing purposes only. For production deployment, we recommend generating a custom Docker image as described in the section [Custom Docker Cassandra image for Magic Collaboration Studio](#InstallingCassandraonWindows-CustomDockerCassandraimageforTeamworkCloud).

Start an instance of the official Cassandra 4.1 Docker image by executing this command:

```bash

```

| Parameter | Value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| --name | cassandra4_test | User-defined container instance name. This is an optional parameter. The name must be unique and cannot be the same as the official Docker image name. |
| -v | e:/cassandra/data/:/var/lib/cassandra | Mount an external drive to a path in the Docker container instance. /var/lib/cassandra is the default Cassandra path for data and commit log storage. An external drive must be mounted for permanent data storage. |
| -d |  | Detach and run in the background. |
| -p | 9042:9042 | Publish port 9042 to Windows host. 9042 is the native client port. |
| [image name]:version | cassandra:4.1.3 | Pull the official Apache Cassandra 4.1 Docker image for this running instance. |

If this is a new Docker installation, the first **docker run** execution downloads the Cassandra image to the local system.

To access logs:

```bash
]]>
```

To access nodetool (or other tools):

```bash
nodetool status]]>
```

Newer Java versions require the **-Dcom.sun.jndi.rmiURLParsing=legacy** option. If the “Malformed IPv6 address” error occurs, use:

```bash
nodetool -Dcom.sun.jndi.rmiURLParsing=legacy status]]>
```

Docker instances are ephemeral by design. If an instance terminates or is deleted, all data, logs, and configuration files modified inside the instance are lost.

##### Custom Docker Cassandra image for Magic Collaboration Studio

Production deployment of Cassandra 4.1 on Windows requires modifying system configurations. Therefore, a custom Docker image must be created with the configuration changes. This section covers setting up and running a custom Docker image. Details of the configuration changes are discussed in section [Cassandra configuration](#InstallingCassandraonWindows-Cassandraconfiguration).

1. Download and extract the [ZIP file](https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/137986348/CustomTWC_Cassandra4_DockerImg.zip?version=1&modificationDate=1695376234919&api=v2) package into an empty working directory.
2. Execute the following command to build the custom Docker image named *cassandra4_twc*: bashDJangotrue
3. Start an instance of the custom Docker Cassandra 4.1 image you just built: bashDJangotrue The data and commit log directories are mounted separately, to different paths.

With the custom Docker Cassandra instance running and mounted to permanent storage, the database is ready to connect to your Magic Collaboration Studio installation.

If more configuration changes are needed, the running instance has to be terminated and a new Docker image needs to be built. Edit the configuration file from the working directory in Step 1. Proceed with steps 2-3 to redeploy the Cassandra node on Windows.

##### Cassandra configuration

This section is a summary of the configuration changes to optimize Docker Cassandra deployment for Magic Collaboration Studio. If additional configuration changes are needed, the Docker Cassandra image has to be rebuilt. All configuration files are located in */etc/cassandra*.

Synchronize the CPU clocks on all Cassandra cluster nodes. Otherwise, you may encounter issues when creating an empty Cassandra cluster.

###### Modification of the *cassandra.yaml* file

If the new Cassandra 4.x node is going to access existing data from a Cassandra 3.x database, please make a note of the previous **cluster_name** and **num_tokens**settings. These parameters must match in the new Cassandra 4.1 *cassandra.yaml* configuration.

The following parameters are for controlling thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly.

```bash

```

```bash

```

Ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended).

```bash

```

```bash

```

Point the data to the appropriate locations.

```text

```

###### JVM configuration for Cassandra Node

To change the amount of RAM used by Cassandra, activate the -Xms4G and -Xmx4g properties in *jvm-server.options* (remove leading ‘#’) and specify their values. By default, Cassandra uses no more than 8 GB of RAM.

- In the jvm11-server.options and jvm8-server.options files, CMS Settings are disabled.
- In the jvm11-server.options and jvm8-server.options files, the parameters below are activated

```text

```

- In the jvm11-server.options and jvm8-server.options files, the two parameters below are set to the physical CPU core count (the values of both parameters should be the same):

```text

```

- In the jvm8-server.options file, disable all GC logging options.
- In the logback.xml file, disable debug.log by commenting out "<appender-ref ref="ASYNCDEBUGLOG" />"

ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker build –t cassandra4_twc:latest .]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p><span>Start an instance of the custom Docker Cassandra 4.1 image you just built:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="3904ca1e-101c-4e70-aa0f-15bb864a354f" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker run –p 9042:9042 –v d:\data:/data –v e:\log\commitlog:/logs/commitlog -d cassandra4_twc:latest]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="4106f393-3cdb-47a7-8d9c-657730c6316c" ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The data and commit log directories are mounted separately, to different paths.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p If additional configuration changes are needed, the Docker Cassandra image has to be rebuilt. All configuration files are located in <em>/etc/cassandra</em>.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="44bae19f-b953-4a3b-92fe-e38c5a186636" ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:rich-text-body>Synchronize the CPU clocks on all Cassandra cluster nodes. Otherwise, you may encounter issues when creating an empty Cassandra cluster.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4><span>Modification of the <em>cassandra.yaml</em> file</span></h4><p><span>If the new Cassandra 4.x node is going to access existing data from a Cassandra 3.x database, please make a note of the previous <strong>cluster_name</strong> and <strong>num_tokens </strong>settings. These parameters must match in the new Cassandra 4.1 <em>cassandra.yaml</em> configuration.</span></p><p><span>The following parameters are for controlling thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="03fbd01c-5306-462b-8149-9fd58fc1fcb1" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example (Cassandra 4.0)</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_segment_size_in_mb: 192
````html
read_request_timeout_in_ms: 1800000
range_request_timeout_in_ms: 1800000
write_request_timeout_in_ms: 1800000
cas_contention_timeout_in_ms: 1000
truncate_request_timeout_in_ms: 1800000
request_timeout_in_ms: 1800000
batch_size_warn_threshold_in_kb: 3000
batch_size_fail_threshold_in_kb: 5000]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="4ea39d10-ff91-4894-b2ef-0cfc8f3ce976" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example (Cassandra 4.1)</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_segment_size: 192MiB
read_request_timeout: 1800000ms
range_request_timeout: 1800000ms
write_request_timeout: 1800000ms
cas_contention_timeout: 1000ms
truncate_request_timeout: 1800000ms
request_timeout: 1800000ms
batch_size_warn_threshold: 3000KiB
batch_size_fail_threshold: 5000KiB]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span>Ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended).</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="cc0b951f-a2a4-4aa4-ab91-576995495f7f" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example (Cassandra 4.0)</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_total_space_in_mb: 8192]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="316545b4-5b92-41e3-9e3a-4b782d47f5ec" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example (Cassandra 4.1)</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_total_space: 8192MiB]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span>Point the data to the appropriate locations.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="3e09c508-0aca-4a7f-9f34-32c24dbc41a4" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[data_file_directories:
- /data/data
commitlog_directory: /logs/commitlog
hints_directory: /data/hints
saved_caches_directory: /data/saved_caches]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>JVM configuration for Cassandra Node</h4><p>To change the amount of RAM used by Cassandra, activate the -Xms4G and -Xmx4g properties in <em>jvm-server.options</em> (remove leading ‘#’) and specify their values. By default, Cassandra uses no more than 8 GB of RAM.</p><ul><li>In the <em>jvm11-server.options </em>and <em>jvm8-server.options</em> files, CMS Settings are disabled.</li><li>In the <em>jvm11-server.options </em>and <em>jvm8-server.options</em> files, the parameters below are activated</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="3c10ca81-73af-4413-8d87-84ed98ffffba" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-XX:+UseG1GC
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=500]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li>In the <em>jvm11-server.options </em>and <em>jvm8-server.options</em> files, the two parameters below are set to the physical CPU core count (the values of both parameters should be the same):</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="9d8bb447-5bfe-41d2-838c-176e83bf2c25" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-XX:ParallelGCThreads=16
-XX:ConcGCThreads=16]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li>In the <em>jvm8-server.options </em>file, disable all GC logging options.</li><li>In the logback.xml file, disable debug.log by commenting out &quot;&lt;appender-ref ref=&quot;ASYNCDEBUGLOG&quot; /&gt;&quot;</li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

## PAGE 00145: Installing Magic Collaboration Studio on Windows

- page_id: `191924209`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section provides instructions on installing Magic Collaboration Studio using either the installer or the zip file. 
Once the installation is complete, you need to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying Magic Collaboration Studio license' space=''] before starting to use Magic Collaboration Studio with the modeling tool and Magic Collaboration Studio Admin.

**IMPORTANT**. To use modeling tools with DSLS licenses,Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable must be installed (see[Latest supported Visual C++ Redistributable downloads](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/windows/latest-supported-vc-redist?view=msvc-170#visual-studio-2015-2017-2019-and-2022)). This applies to Windows OS only.

The following sections provide instructions on setting up Magic Collaboration Studio on Windows using the installer or the zip file.
